source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Recognise that living things have changed over time and that fossils provide information about living things that inhabited the Earth millions of years ago; recognise that living things produce offspring of the same kind, but normally offspring vary and are not identical to their parents; identify how animals and plants are adapted to suit their environment in different ways and that adaptation may lead to evolution.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack builds a causal evidence chain from inherited variation and environmental advantage to population change over many generations, while using fossils as evidence of change through time.

objective.child_goal: I can explain how variation, inheritance and adaptation can change populations over time.
objective.statement: Use evidence from offspring, adaptations and fossils to explain how inherited variation can lead to changes in populations over many generations.
misconceptions: Thinks an individual organism changes its inherited features because it needs to adapt.
Thinks offspring are exact copies of their parents or inherit every acquired characteristic.
Treats any useful feature as an adaptation without linking it to survival, reproduction and inheritance.
Thinks fossils show a complete, perfectly ordered record or that humans lived alongside all extinct organisms.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The passing of characteristics from parents to offspring.
vocabulary.audio_script: Inheritance is how some characteristics pass from parents to offspring.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Trait threads connecting parent and offspring cards.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Differences between individuals of the same kind.
vocabulary.audio_script: Variation means individuals in a population are not all identical.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A population row with measurable differences highlighted.

vocabulary.child_explanation: An inherited characteristic that helps an organism survive and reproduce in its environment.
vocabulary.audio_script: An adaptation is an inherited feature that gives an advantage in a particular environment.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A feature card linked to environment, survival and offspring.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Change in inherited characteristics of a population over many generations.
vocabulary.audio_script: Evolution is population change across many generations.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A multi-generation population timeline changing in proportions.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Preserved remains, impressions or traces of organisms from the past.
vocabulary.audio_script: Fossils are evidence of organisms that lived long ago.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A fossil layer card connected to an evidence note.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A group of the same kind of organism living in an area.
vocabulary.audio_script: A population is a group of the same kind living in one area.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Many individual organisms enclosed by one habitat boundary.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which features vary among these individuals of the same species?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Retrieve observation and classification while establishing within-species variation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Members of the same species share many features, but they are not identical.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A population sample with measurable features such as colour shade, height and beak depth.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Follow a population, not one individual, across many generations.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Establish the scale and timespan of evolutionary change.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Individuals live and reproduce. Populations can change across generations.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A timeline separates one organism's lifetime from repeated population generations.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: In a dry habitat, some plants inherit deeper roots. They are more likely to survive drought and leave offspring with the root variation.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model the causal chain variation, inheritance, advantage, reproduction and population change.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The plants do not grow deep roots because they try. Some already vary, and inherited helpful variation becomes more common over generations.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Trait proportions change across four generations while rainfall stays low.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Change the environment, predict which inherited variation gives an advantage, then run several generations.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Use a model to connect environment-specific advantage with changing trait frequency.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Predict first, run the model, then explain what changed in the population.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A population simulation displays trait counts before and after reproduction.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which claim is supported by these fossils and rock-layer dates?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Diagnose whether the learner distinguishes evidence-supported inference from overclaim.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Fossils give evidence, but gaps in the record mean we should not claim more than the evidence shows.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Fossil cards align with dated rock layers and claim cards.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach the coach why a single animal does not evolve a new inherited feature during its lifetime.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Repair need-driven and individual-change misconceptions through causal explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Use the words variation, inherited, survive, reproduce and generations.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The coach points to one animal while the learner redirects the explanation to a population timeline.

manipulatives.purpose: Make population-level change visible by tracking inherited trait proportions across repeated generations.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners choose an environment, predict advantage, run generations, compare trait counts and build a causal explanation from the model.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Use patterns plus labels rather than colour alone, provide a data-table alternative, keyboard and switch controls, reduced-motion step frames and a simplified-count SEND view that preserves the same causal reasoning.

manipulatives.purpose: Distinguish inherited variation, environmental effects and acquired changes without implying a simple one-gene model.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners sort carefully worded characteristic evidence, compare related individuals and justify when evidence is insufficient.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Read cards aloud, limit simultaneous choices, use familiar non-sensitive examples and allow oral or selected justification.

manipulatives.purpose: Use fossils and relative or supplied dates as incomplete evidence for organisms and environments in the past.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners order layers, connect fossil evidence to cautious claims and identify where the record has gaps.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Provide tactile-style outlines, high-contrast layer labels, a list view and no requirement to infer from colour or decorative detail.

practice.interleaving_notes: Teach inheritance and variation before selection models; interleave fossil evidence only after learners can distinguish present-day mechanism from evidence of past change.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Recognise shared inherited characteristics alongside normal variation among offspring.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: parent and offspring evidence cards + inherited/environmental/insufficient-evidence categories
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid deterministic claims about complex human traits and avoid sensitive family-comparison contexts.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Build explanations linking inherited variation to survival, reproduction and population change.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: environment + existing variation + differential outcome + causal explanation choices
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Every correct explanation must refer to populations and generations, not purposeful individual change.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Predict and interpret changes in inherited trait frequency across several generations.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: two or three trait variants + environmental condition + generation counts + before/after data
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Label simulations as simplified models and vary which trait is advantageous by environment.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Evaluate cautious claims about past organisms and change using incomplete fossil evidence.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: dated or ordered rock layers + fossil features + supported, unsupported and overclaimed statements
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Scientific review must check chronology and avoid presenting a ladder of progress.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain vocabulary and causal reasoning through short spaced-review explanations.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review queue selects variation, inheritance, adaptation, population or fossil evidence prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use low-reading-load prompts and rotate visual, data and verbal evidence.

body.prompt: A litter of fox cubs has the same species features as its parents, but the cubs differ slightly in coat shade and body size. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: The cubs are the same kind as their parents but show normal variation.
The cubs must be exact copies of one parent.
The cubs changed species because they look different.
Every difference was learned after birth.
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: The cubs are the same kind as their parents but show normal variation.

hints: Offspring of the same kind share characteristics.
Individuals within a species are not usually identical.
question_variants.explanation: The cubs inherit species characteristics from their parents while also showing variation in features such as shade and size.

feedback.correct: Correct. The cubs inherit species characteristics from their parents while also showing variation in features such as shade and size.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: Offspring of the same kind share characteristics. Individuals within a species are not usually identical. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the offspring exact copy route: Offspring of the same kind share characteristics. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the offspring exact copy idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: A plant population already varies in root depth. During repeated droughts, deeper-rooted plants leave more offspring, and root depth is inherited. What is the best prediction after many generations?
choices: Deep-root variation is likely to become more common in the population.
Each shallow-rooted plant will choose to grow inherited deep roots.
All plants will become identical in one generation.
Drought will directly rewrite every plant to the same root depth.
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: Deep-root variation is likely to become more common in the population.

hints: The variation exists before the drought.
Which plants are more likely to survive and pass on the inherited variation?
question_variants.explanation: If deeper roots give an inherited survival and reproductive advantage during drought, that variation can become more common across generations.

feedback.correct: Correct. If deeper roots give an inherited survival and reproductive advantage during drought, that variation can become more common across generations.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: The variation exists before the drought. Which plants are more likely to survive and pass on the inherited variation? No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the need driven change route: The variation exists before the drought. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the need driven change idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: Older rock layers contain fossils of a shelled sea organism with one chamber shape. Younger layers contain related fossils with a modified chamber shape. Which claim is best supported?
choices: Related organisms with different chamber forms lived at different times.
The fossil record contains every individual that ever lived.
One fossil changed its chamber after it was buried.
Humans observed both organisms making the fossils.
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: Related organisms with different chamber forms lived at different times.

hints: Use only what the fossils and layers support.
Avoid claims about a complete record or changes after burial.
question_variants.explanation: The layer order supports the cautious claim that related forms lived at different times; it does not make the fossil record complete.

feedback.correct: Correct. The layer order supports the cautious claim that related forms lived at different times; it does not make the fossil record complete.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: Use only what the fossils and layers support. Avoid claims about a complete record or changes after burial. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the fossil record complete route: Use only what the fossils and layers support. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the fossil record complete idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

body.prompt: Why can pale fur be an adaptation for a predator living on snow?
choices: If fur colour is inherited, pale individuals may be less visible, catch more prey and leave more offspring.
Every predator decides to turn pale when snow falls.
Pale fur is always helpful in every environment.
The snow teaches each predator's fur to change permanently.
body.supported_interaction: Use labelled touch or keyboard controls. Equivalent routes include single-switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell selection, AAC, pointing, partner or adult-supported response; fine dragging, handwriting and speech are never required.

expected_answer.value: If fur colour is inherited, pale individuals may be less visible, catch more prey and leave more offspring.

hints: An adaptation is inherited and useful in a particular environment.
Link the feature to survival or reproduction.
question_variants.explanation: Pale fur can be an adaptation on snow when inherited camouflage improves hunting success and reproductive success; the advantage depends on the environment.

feedback.correct: Correct. Pale fur can be an adaptation on snow when inherited camouflage improves hunting success and reproductive success; the advantage depends on the environment.
feedback.retry: Try again calmly: An adaptation is inherited and useful in a particular environment. Link the feature to survival or reproduction. No progress is lost for checking or changing your answer.
feedback.repair: Repair the feature useful everywhere route: An adaptation is inherited and useful in a particular environment. Then use the evidence to explain your choice.
feedback.misconception_check: Check the feature useful everywhere idea against the task evidence.
feedback.support_message: Touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing and adult-supported routes are valid; speed, speech and fine-motor precision are not scored.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks an individual organism changes its inherited features because it needs to adapt.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner says an organism deliberately develops a heritable feature during its lifetime in response to need.
repair_sequence: Separate one lifetime from a multi-generation population timeline.
Show variation already present before the environmental pressure.
Track which variants leave more offspring and how population proportions change.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains a new example using pre-existing inherited variation and population change over generations.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks offspring are exact copies of their parents or inherit every acquired characteristic.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner predicts identical offspring or classifies a learned or injury-related change as necessarily inherited.
repair_sequence: Compare several offspring from the same parents and identify shared features and differences.
Sort evidence into inherited, environmental and not enough information.
Explain that inheritance contributes to characteristics without making offspring identical.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner identifies normal variation among related offspring and avoids claiming that an acquired change must be inherited.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats any useful feature as an adaptation without linking it to survival, reproduction and inheritance.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner labels a temporary behaviour or universally useful feature as an adaptation without environmental context.
repair_sequence: Ask whether the characteristic is inherited.
Name the particular environment and test whether the feature gives an advantage there.
Link the advantage to greater survival or reproductive success.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner justifies an adaptation with inheritance, environment and reproductive consequence.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks fossils show a complete, perfectly ordered record or that humans lived alongside all extinct organisms.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner treats missing fossils as proof of absence, claims every organism fossilised or ignores rock-layer chronology.
repair_sequence: Model the rare conditions needed for fossilisation.
Mark gaps and uncertainty in a sequence of fossil evidence.
Match only cautious claims to the evidence and reject unsupported chronology claims.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner uses fossil evidence to make a bounded claim and explicitly recognises a gap or limitation.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm field-lab visuals, stable population counts and no distressing survival imagery.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Show generations as stepped data panels rather than animated births, deaths or rapid population movement.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate causal chains and fossil evidence with replayable chunks and explicit time-language.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use short claim cards, labelled diagrams, vocabulary popovers and side-by-side before/after population tables.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Keep one causal link visible at a time: variation, inheritance, advantage, reproduction, population change.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow prediction before simulation, freeze each generation and provide a causal-chain organiser.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat predictions as testable scientific ideas and separate model interpretation from recall of terminology.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach inherit, vary, adapt, population, generation, fossil and evidence with visuals; contrast everyday and scientific meanings of adapt.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires recognising inherited variation, linking an adaptation to a particular environment and interpreting a simple multi-generation model; secure requires explaining population change without need-driven language and evaluating the limits of fossil evidence.
evidence.parent_summary: Can explain how inherited differences, environmental pressures and evidence from fossils help scientists understand change over many generations.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Distinguishes individual and population change, builds a variation-to-reproduction causal chain, interprets trait-frequency data and makes appropriately cautious fossil-based claims.
next_actions: Interleave classification evidence and working-scientifically evaluation of models.
Compare environments where the same variation has different advantages.
Bridge to Year 7 genetics language only after inheritance is understood without deterministic oversimplification.

qa.notes: Year 6 evolution-and-inheritance pilot reaches 240 variants with four curated questions preserved semantically unchanged and 236 deterministic review candidates. Coverage follows a causal progression from within-species variation and careful inheritance evidence to environment-specific adaptation, differential survival/reproduction and population trait-frequency change across generations, then fossils and dated/ordered layers as incomplete evidence. Need-driven change, individual-evolves, exact-copy, every-useful-feature-is-adaptation and complete-fossil-record misconceptions are explicitly repaired. All four declared formats and five blueprints include patterned/labelled population tables, inheritance sorts, fossil investigations and causal explain choices, with simplified-count SEND, static, keyboard, switch and no-drag alternatives. Selected narration references require produced, human-reviewed ElevenLabs assets; browser TTS is prohibited. Independent science, teacher, SEND, accessibility, safeguarding, audio and renderer review remains required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
